data.title: Eisai's Alzheimer Drug Leqembi Receives EU Marketing Authorization
data.description:
Eisai Co and Biogen said the European Commission has granted marketing authorization for Leqembi (lecanemab) in the European Union, according to a statement on Wednesday.

This antibody therapy is the first in the EU to target an underlying cause of Alzheimer's disease.

Leqembi is indicated for adults with mild cognitive impairment and mild dementia due to Alzheimer's who have specific genetic profiles and confirmed amyloid pathology.

The drug uniquely binds to and clears toxic amyloid-beta protofibrils and reduces amyloid plaques, addressing a key driver of neuronal injury in early Alzheimer's.

data.title: Market Chatter: Japan Ends Gasoline Subsidies as Oil Prices Fall, Yen Strengthens
data.description:
Japan's gasoline subsidies will fall to zero yen per liter this week, a decrease from 4.40 yen the previous week due to lower crude oil prices and a stronger yen, according to Nippon.com, citing the industry ministry.

This marks the first time the subsidy has been eliminated since its introduction in January 2022, as prices are expected to drop below the 185 yen threshold, triggering the support program, said the news portal.

Initially a temporary COVID-19 recovery measure, the subsidy program has been extended multiple times and has so far cost over 8 trillion yen, the report added.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Rio Tinto Group Working With Canadian Firm to Develop Technology for Bauxite Residue Valorization
data.description:
Rio Tinto Group and Geomega Resources are collaborating to develop the bauxite residue valorization technology, Geomega said in a statement Wednesday.

Rio Tinto provided Geomega with a specific range of parameters for purities and particle sizes, which have now been successfully confirmed and validated by Rio Tinto's team.

The technology is divided into three consecutive circuits, which are being tested and scaled up individually.

The first circuit focuses on alkalinity removal and the production of several important off-take streams such as sodium, calcium, aluminum, and silica products, Geomega said. The second circuit deals with iron extraction and removal. The third circuit deals with the remaining high-value metals, and Geomega is currently developing a cost-efficient circuit to extract and purify a rare earths concentrate as well as a titanium concentrate.

Pilot test work has been completed for the first and second circuits, which has allowed the Canadian firm to produce a representative sample of the residue needed for the piloting of the third circuit, which is planned for 2025.

Geomega and Rio Tinto have signed an extension to their collaboration agreement to continue the work on the third circuit as part of the project that was launched in March 2022.

In parallel, Geomega and Rio Tinto are planning the next phase for the first and second circuits based on the encouraging results.

Rio Tinto's shares rose nearly 2% in recent trading on Thursday.

data.title: New Zealand's Tourism Holdings slumps on seeing lower-than-expected FY profit

data.title: Singapore Shares Rise 0.6%, Buoyed by Prospects of U.S.-China Trade TalksMarket Talk
data.description: Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index rises 0.6% to 3683.45 after earlier swinging between mild losses and gains, buoyed by prospects of U.S.-China trade talks. There's a report that "China wants to see a number of steps from President Trump's administration before it will agree to trade talks," MUFG Bank's Michael Wan says in a research report. These steps include a "more consistent U.S. position," the senior currency analyst adds. Among the best performers on the benchmark index, DFI Retail Group rises 4.5%, Jardine Matheson Holdings adds 3.5%, and City Developments is 2.4% higher. Meanwhile, Yangzijiang Shipbuilding falls 1.0% and Wilmar International is down 1.0%.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0209 GMT - Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index rises 0.6% to 3683.45 after earlier swinging between mild losses and gains, buoyed by prospects of U.S.-China trade talks. There's a report that "China wants to see a number of steps from President Trump's administration before it will agree to trade talks," MUFG Bank's Michael Wan says in a research report. These steps include a "more consistent U.S. position," the senior currency analyst adds. Among the best performers on the benchmark index, DFI Retail Group rises 4.5%, Jardine Matheson Holdings adds 3.5%, and City Developments is 2.4% higher. Meanwhile, Yangzijiang Shipbuilding falls 1.0% and Wilmar International is down 1.0%.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0205 GMT - Gamuda's growing renewable energy business in Australia is likely to support its long-term growth on the back of Australia's green transition, RHB Investment Bank analyst Adam Bin Mohamed Rahim says in a note. Its unit DT Infrastructure's early work for the Carmody's Hill Wind Farm project in South Australia may pave the way for larger construction contracts. Meanwhile, Gamuda's construction revenue growth in Malaysia is likely driven by data center, rail and hydropower projects, potentially supporting higher net margins, the analyst says. RHB maintains a buy rating on Gamuda and keeps its target price at MYR5.83. Shares are 0.3% lower at MYR3.88.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0155 GMT - Chinese shares are mixed in early trade. The Shanghai Composite Index is down 0.1% to 3272.25, the Shenzhen Composite Index is up 0.3% and the ChiNext Price Index is also 0.3% higher. Investors are digesting China's solid 1Q GDP data and watching any developments in the U.S.-China trade tensions. Among the major stocks, banks are leading the declines. Bank of China is down 1.2% and China CITIC Bank is 2.3% lower. Property stocks edge higher, with Poly Developments & Holdings rising 2.4% and China Merchants Shekou Industrial Zone Holdings 2.6% higher. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0147 GMT - Hong Kong shares rise, supported by property and auto stocks. The benchmark Hang Seng Index is up 0.5% at 21168.24 and the Hang Seng Tech Index gains 0.2%. Investors are watching for any developments in the U.S.-China trade spat and if Beijing will release stimulus measures to boost its economy. Longfor Group leads gains, up 3.5%, while China Resources Land adds 1.7%. Li Auto rises 2.25%. Meanwhile, China Mengniu Dairy leads losses, down 2.5%. Meituan and Xiaomi fall 2.15% and 0.7%, respectively.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0130 GMT - Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index edges 0.2% lower to 3656.57, tracking Wall Street's losses overnight. The main theme has been risk-off, Commerzbank Research analysts say in a report. The U.S. has imposed new restriction on exports of AI chips to China, the analysts say. Among the worst performers, Venture Corp. falls 1.5%, Yangzijiang Shipbuilding drops 2.9% and Seatrium is down 1.6%. Meanwhile, Jardine Matheson Holdings rises 2.6%, DFI Retail Group adds 2.7% and Hongkong Land is up 1.2%.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0119 GMT - China's palm oil demand may rise amid the U.S.-China trade tensions as the Asian country seeks alternatives for soybean imports from the U.S., says Hong Leong Investment Bank analyst Chye Wen Fei in a note. However, downside risks from weak global demand and falling crude oil prices could offset any gains in crude palm oil, she says. Lower crude oil prices reduce the appeal of palm oil as biofuel. She maintains her 2025-2026 estimates for crude palm oil prices at MYR4,000 a ton and MYR3,800 a ton, respectively. With palm oil supply recovering and no strong catalysts in sight, Chye keeps a neutral rating on the Malaysian plantation sector. Hong Leong names Johor Plantations, Hap Seng Plantations and IOI Corp. as its top sector picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0105 GMT - Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index is 0.1% lower at 1475.44. Following President Xi's visit, Malaysia and China struck 31 MOUs across key sectors, which could lift market sentiment, Malacca Securities says in a note. Tourism, plantations, tech and construction may see near-term gains, the brokerage reckons. Among decliners, YTL Power International and YTL Corp. are each down 2.2%. Meanwhile, Petronas Gas and Telekom Malaysia both add 0.6%.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0032 GMT - South32's 3Q production was in line with market expectations with the exception of its Cannington mine, Jefferies analyst Mitch Ryan says in a note. "While we expect pricing for sales to remain somewhat volatile with the overhang of tariffs and shifting global supply chains, FX tailwinds and stable operating conditions suggest H2 margins should remain relatively robust," Ryan says. Jefferies has a hold rating and A$3.20 target on the stock, which is down 0.8% at A$2.65. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0026 GMT - BHP reported robust 3Q output and, importantly, has been able to find a way to avoid the sharp reduction in Escondida output beyond FY27, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart. "We don't have details but it includes extending the life of the oldest concentrator beyond FY29," he says in a note. That's "a good outcome for Escondida following the disappointments of the November site visit last year," McTaggart adds. Citi has a buy rating and A$45.00 target on BHP, which is up 0.5% at A$36.24. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0021 GMT - Japanese stocks are higher as some bargain hunting kicks in following their recent declines despite continuing uncertainty over U.S. tariffs. Electronics and heavy industry stocks are leading gains. TDK is up 3.0% and Lasertec is 1.5% higher while Kawasaki Heavy Industries is up 2.4% and IHI is 1.4% higher. USD/JPY is at 142.28, compared with 142.36 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any tariff-related news ahead of the earnings season set to start next week. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.2% at 33986.96. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0020 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi rises 0.3% to 2454.81 in early trade, as defense and shipbuilding stocks advance. The market impact from new U.S. trade restrictions on Nvidia's chips is easing as local stocks priced in much of it a day earlier, Kiwoom Securities says in a note. Trading volume is thin ahead of the Bank of Korea's rate decision later in the day. Fighter-jet maker Korea Aerospace Industries rises 2.6%. Shipbuilder HD Hyundai Heavy Industries gains 2.8%. USD/KRW is 0.5% lower at 1,419.25 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield is down 3.5 bps at 2.629%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2342 GMT - Japanese stocks may decline as uncertainty persists over U.S. tariffs and their economic implications. Nikkei futures are down 0.4% at 33820 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 141.69, down from 142.36 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any tariff-related news ahead of the earnings season set to start next week. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1.0% to 33920.40 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

data.title: Asian Stocks Rise as U.S.-Japan Trade Talks Kick Off
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

Asian equity markets gained Thursday amid positive signals from U.S. equity futures as investors await the outcome of U.S.-Japan trade talks.

"Being one of the first countries to approach the U.S. for direct talks, Japan's discussions are being closely watched by financial markets to assess what concessions are discussed at the meeting," ANZ Research said in a note.

The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.6%, with electronics and heavy industry stocks leading gains. TDK added 3.3% and Advantest gained 2.1%, while Kawasaki Heavy Industries advanced 3.5%.

South Korea's Kospi was up 0.35%, shortly after the Bank of Korea's decision to keep interest rates steady amid U.S. tariff uncertainties, while Australia's S&P/ASX 200 rose 0.3%.

Meanwhile, Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index rose 0.5%, but China's Shanghai Composite Index ticked down 0.1%.

President Trump's exclusion of levies on consumer electronics gave investors a brief reprieve earlier this week, with stocks broadly gaining across the region. However, the lack of clarity on looming tariff developments continues to weigh on sentiment as markets struggle to digest changing tit-for-tat tariff developments.

Analysts think U.S. tariff hikes on Japan could remain suspended indefinitely even after the current 90-day pause.

However, automobile tariffs and other industry-specific levies are likely here to stay, though there is still "quite a high degree of uncertainty," Nomura economists said in a note.

Spot gold recently touched another record intraday high of $3,357.92 a troy ounce, and was last down 0.4% at $3,328.07 a troy ounce.

The U.S. dollar rose 0.5% against the Japanese yen.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: Bitcoin Supply In Profit Hasn’t Lost This Key Level Yet—Bull Cycle Intact?
data.description:
On-chain data shows the Bitcoin Supply in Profit is yet to drop under this key level during the current cycle, a sign that could be optimistic for BTC.

Bitcoin Supply in Profit Has Remained Above 70% So Far

In a CryptoQuant Quicktake post, an analyst has talked about the the recent trend in the Supply in Profit for Bitcoin. The “Supply in Profit” is an indicator that keeps track of the percentage of the total BTC supply in circulation that’s being held at a net unrealized profit.

The metric works by looking at the transaction history of each coin in the circulating supply to see what price it was last moved at. The previous transaction of any token is likely to represent the last point at which it changed hands, so the price at that time would denote its current cost basis.

Naturally, if this acquisition level for a given coin is under the latest spot price, then that particular coin would be considered to be holding some net gain. The Supply in Profit adds up all tokens satisfying this condition and determines what part of the supply they make up for.

Another indicator called the Supply in Loss deals with the tokens of the opposite type. This indicator’s value can also be found by subtracting the Supply in Profit from 100, since both of the metrics must add up to 100%.

Now, here is the chart for the Bitcoin Supply in Profit shared by the quant that shows the trend in its value for the last several years:

As is visible in the above graph, the Bitcoin Supply in Profit hit the 100% mark earlier as the cryptocurrency’s price explored new all-time highs (ATHs). With the drawdown that has occurred during the last few months, however, the indicator has plunged.

In the chart, the quant has highlighted two lines that could prove to be of relevance to the asset. The bottom one (shaded in orange) corresponds to a value of 70%. It would appear that during the last cycle, losing this level meant the start of a bear market for BTC.

So far in the current cycle, Bitcoin is yet to see a drop below it. The closest it came was during last year’s consolidation phase. A level that the asset has dropped under a few times already this cycle is the 80% one, colored in yellow.

The recent correction also took the Supply in Profit below it, but the recovery rally has meant that it’s close to retesting the line again. During both the previous and current cycles, the initial break above the line signaled the start of the bull market for the coin.

Based on the pattern, the analyst has noted, “next target is to push the supply in profit back to 80% to signal strong bullish momentum.”

BTC Price

Bitcoin has retraced some of its recent recovery during the past day as its price has slipped back down to $83,900.

data.title: REP. RO KHANNA (D-CA) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 16, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

REP. RO KHANNA (D-CA)

REP. RO KHANNA (D-CA) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

REP. RO KHANNA (D-CA) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

APRIL 16, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. RO KHANNA (D-CA)

SYMONE SANDERS-TOWNSEND, MSNBC HOST: Joining me now is Congressman Ro Khanna of California and Melissa Murray, NYU law professor, MSNBC legal analyst and co-host of the "Strict Scrutiny" podcast.

I literally — I mean, honestly, I have had it just as much as it seems like Judge Boasberg in this 46-page directive, if you will.

Congressman, can I just start with you?

We are seeing at least one of the guardrails that our founding fathers intended on working. That is the judiciary. Your thoughts about the developments today and, frankly, the fact that the Trump administration is actively defying other court orders as we speak.

REP. RO KHANNA (D-CA): It's a betrayal of the American experiment.

Look, in J.D. Vance and Donald Trump's America, police can knock on an immigrant's door, deport them to a dictatorship without any due process. Now, the Supreme Court, 9-0, said that Abrego is entitled to due process here. It's important to realize it's not that El Salvador came and kidnapped him. We sent him there.

We can certainly demand that he return. And J.D. Vance exposed everything when he tweeted out saying he's not even concerned about a pretense of compliance with the Supreme Court. He just believes that immigrants should not get due process.

Well, you have a sitting vice president who is saying to defy the Supreme Court and saying that the Supreme Court's interpretation of the Constitution and due process is irrelevant and all that matters is what he thinks. This is an unprecedented crisis in our country's history.

SANDERS-TOWNSEND: Unprecedented crisis.

Melissa, you're a constitutional law expert here. A key quote from Boasberg's ruling, he noted: "Defendants offer various imaginative arguments for why they nevertheless technically complied with the order. None of their positions would stand scrutiny."

He also writes: "Defendants' protest that the Supreme Court has since vacated the TROs. How can contempt lie? The Supreme Court long ago answered that question. It's firmly settled that a court order must be obeyed until it is reversed for error by the issuing court."

Make it make sense, Melissa.

MELISSA MURRAY, MSNBC LEGAL ANALYST: Sure.

So, Judge Boasberg in that passage is referring to the fact that the Supreme Court actually vacated his initial order on a procedural ground, that the case should have been filed in the Fifth Circuit, where the detainees had actually been before they were then rendered to El Salvador.

So they took it out of Judge Boasberg's chambers and moved it to that other court. But the point he is making is that, before that happened, before the Supreme Court acted, he had issued this order to the government to ask them to provide those detainees with due process, to let them have an opportunity to explain whether they had been removed in error or whether these charges against them were in error.

And that order had to stand and they had to comply with it until the Supreme Court removed it and put it into another court. So that's the point that he's making there. But I just want to address this broader question about the courts here.

The courts are doing a Herculean job right now standing up to this administration, but it's not really the job that they were intended to do, or at least not what they were intended to do alone. This is a tripartite system of government. The judiciary is just one branch. The other branch is Congress.

And I would say here, with all due respect to Congressman Khanna, who I know from law school, Congress has completely abdicated the field here. And I know that the Democrats are not in a majority here, but this is a situation where a president is acting so lawlessly that Congress should be empowered to act, whether it is through oversight, whether it is through the power of the purse.

Turn off the lights in the White House until there is compliance with the judicial order. There are lots of things that Congress can do if it has the will to do it. It is acting on behalf of the American people. And that is the question here too.

SANDERS-TOWNSEND: Congressman, I guess is there anything you think that would move your Republican colleagues to assert what is their constitutional authority to do their jobs as being a co-equal branch of government?

They are currently acting like an extension of the executive branch.

KHANNA: The one place that they started to have doubts is on the capricious tariff policy, because the one thing in America you can't do is destroy people's money and wealth.

And you have had some Republicans speak up. Their constituents' retirement plans have been hurt. The farmers have been hurt. Small businesses have been hurt. But what I believe, and Melissa's absolutely right, is that Congress needs to act.

Look, when Brown v. Board of Education came out, the Southerners, in a shameful moment of history in Congress, passed a House resolution — or didn't pass it, introduced it, condemning Board — Brown v. Board of Education. It was, unfortunately, a very powerful document.

We need to, in my view right now, have a House resolution, make it as bipartisan, saying that the president has to comply with the Supreme Court. And then we need to use the power of the purse to make sure that happens. We also need an emergency DNC meeting that people have called for.

I don't — I think the American people get the urgency. Frankly, I don't think enough people in Congress do. And so I think the critique is valid.

SANDERS-TOWNSEND: As we speak, I am just getting notification that the Trump administration has appealed Judge Boasberg's ruling, his order where he found probable cause to hold the government in criminal contempt. So this will actually continue in court.

Just today, a federal judge has called Donald Trump's order targeting prominent law firms as shocking abuse of power. You couple this with the other lawlessness acts that we have talked about here.

Melissa, it just makes me wonder, if the biggest law firms in this country, many of them unwilling to stand up to the president, if the — some of the richest people in this country, many of them unwilling to stand up to the president and this administration and think it's better to just go along with the get-along and give him what he wants, if lawmakers in the Republican Party think it's — are afraid to stand up to the president, if some Democrats, elected Democrats in the country don't feel as though it is proper to stand up to the president, how the heck are regular people across the country supposed to feel as though that they have any power here, even though they do have the power?

But at this point — moment, it doesn't feel that way.

MURRAY: Yes, it's a great question. There are a lot of people here with lots on the line, whether it is businesses that may go under because of retaliation from this administration, whether it's universities that risk losing crucial funding, whether it is for ordinary operations or for innovative research that will advance humankind going forward.

There's a lot to lose here. And you're right. I think people are trying to make good-faith assessments of how to strike that balance. And it is an unenviable task. The bottom line that I think we all need to remember is that these individuals, whether it's law firms or universities or even members of Congress, I think, to some degree, didn't put themselves in this mess.

This is an administration that is doing this to the American public, retaliating against those whose understanding of the rule of law does not align with its own. And how do ordinary people stand up? I think it is making it clear to your representatives and senators that they don't represent this administration. They represent their constituents.

And maybe some of their constituents want this, but I doubt all of their constituents do. And some of the defunding of the universities is happening in red states, where the university itself is among the largest employer.

So the defunding of the university is going to have a real and material impact on the economies of those cities in Alabama, in other parts of the South, in deep red states. This is going to hurt everyone. It's not just sticking it to elite universities. It's not just sticking it to law firms. Everyone will suffer.

SANDERS-TOWNSEND: I will note that "The New York Times" is reporting today that the law firms made a deal with Trump and now he wants more from them.

We have been talking about — and here it is right there. He wants more from them. If you give a bully just a little bit and you give him your lunch today, he's going to come back for your lunch tomorrow.

As we speak, Senator Chris Van Hollen today traveled to El Salvador. We spoke with him on Monday and he told us those were his plans. He did a press conference while he was there. He met with the vice president of El Salvador.

I want to play for you all a little bit of what he had to say.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

SEN. CHRIS VAN HOLLEN (D-MD): I asked the vice president, if Abrego Garcia has not committed a crime and the U.S. courts have found that he was illegally taken for the United States, and the governor — government of El Salvador has no evidence that he was part of MS-13, why is El Salvador continuing to hold him in CECOT?

And his answer was that the Trump administration is paying El Salvador, the government of El Salvador, to keep him at CECOT.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

SANDERS-TOWNSEND: Congressman, I thought that there was a one-time $6 million payment. Is these — are these new payments? Is this the first you have heard that apparently there are continuous payments being made to the government of El Salvador from the Trump administration?

KHANNA: Well, there's no transparency. We don't know.

But it shows — and I admire Senator Van Hollen's courage. But it shows that the Trump administration is in blatant violation of the Supreme Court. Look, they're saying that they're going to send a plane to get Abrego but it's really Bukele who has the decision. That's simply not true. We realize now that they're paying El Salvador.

Why don't they stop the payment? Why doesn't Trump direct Bukele or demand that be returned? They are playing games when — to avoid a court order. And Melissa is the expert, but my view is that the Supreme Court was too vague, that they need to be more direct and tell the president that he needs to demand the return of Abrego.

SANDERS-TOWNSEND: Congressman, I have a follow-up question for you. Can we switch gears and talk about J.D. Vance?

Because you mentioned the vice president a little bit in your — at the top? And I want to just put on the screen for folks what the vice president of the United States tweeted.

He said: "To say the administration must observe due process is to beg the question, what process is due is function of our resources, the public interest, the status of the accused, the proposed punishment, and so many other factors."

Here's the thing. Here's the thing. What the heck? Is the vice president of the United States really asserting that if someone is a criminal and undocumented and — or undocumented in this country, they do not deserve due process?

KHANNA: You want to talk about word salads? That's a word salad. Let me tell you something.

Vice President Harris would run circles around J.D. Vance's understanding of American law.

SANDERS-TOWNSEND: Well...

KHANNA: It's an embarrassment that this man is vice president and graduated from Yale Law School and puts out stuff like that.

I mean, what J.D. Vance is saying is that the Supreme Court and the Constitution's interpretation of due process doesn't matter, that the police can literally come to any person's home if they're not a U.S. citizen, take them, that the United States government can send them away to a dictatorship and that they don't even need a trial.

And who gets to decide if that's fair? J.D. Vance and Donald Trump. That is what J.D. Vance is arguing for in that tweet. His own attorney general isn't frivolous enough to make that kind of argument. They're at least keeping a pretense that we're going to send a plane.

Vance is saying, no, you don't need to do it. He's basically saying the Supreme Court has ruled. Now let them enforce it. I'm just going to go with my interpretation. It is dangerous and it is a threat to the very constitutional system, not just for Abrego. If they can do it to Abrego, they can do it to any person who's vulnerable in this country.

SANDERS-TOWNSEND: And that right there is the point. This is a test case.

Representative Ro Khanna, thank you for your time. We appreciate you.

END

data.title: Litmus test for chip stocks as investors gird for TSMC earnings
data.description:
Investors are bracing for the results from Taiwan's TSMC on Thursday to show further evidence of the wide-ranging uncertainty in the chip industry spurred by U.S. President Donald Trump's trade policies.

Chip stocks across the globe have suffered this year as investor jitters about spending on AI infrastructure, the threat of competition after Chinese startup DeepSeek's launch, and, in recent weeks, fears around U.S. tariffs have sapped sentiment.

On Wednesday, chip stocks were battered as Nvidia warned of a $5.5 billion hit after Washington restricted exports of its AI processor tailored for China, while Dutch giant ASML raised doubts about its outlook.

The spotlight now shifts to earnings from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co, whose customers include Apple and Nvidia. TSMC is the main producer of advanced chips used in artificial intelligence applications and has been boosted by strong demand in recent years.

The focus will be on capital spending plans as well as comments on demand for AI chips and how TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker, is navigating Trump's back-and-forth tariff challenges, investors say.

"Managements are going to be cautious simply because there's so much uncertainty, but also it's a little bit of get-out-of-jail-free card," said Mark Hackett, chief market strategist at Nationwide in Philadelphia. "If they're nervous about a lot of other things, they can just blame it on the tariff policies and say that there's too much uncertainty and we're not going to give you guidance."

Thursday's results are likely to show a 54% jump in profit for TSMC, but for Gary Tan, a portfolio manager at Allspring Global Investments, what matters are the firm's capital expenditure plans and comments on margins.

Tan, whose fund owns TSMC stocks but is underweight on Taiwan, said TSMC is in the strongest position to pass on price increases.

"That will be a proxy that you can work down the supply chain," he said.

The threat of tariffs has weighed on TSMC stock. The Taipei-listed shares of TSMC are down more than 20% so far in 2025 in its worst start to the year in at least three decades as foreign investors flee. Its U.S. listed shares are down 23%.

Foreign investors have sold $8.66 billion worth of TSMC shares so far this year after buying $2 billion last year and $10.4 billion in 2023, Goldman Sachs said in a report.

The exodus of foreign investors from TSMC and Taiwan stocks through the year underscores the wavering sentiment as traders grapple with Trump's fast-evolving trade policies.

Taiwan had been due to be hit with a 32% tariff by U.S. President Donald Trump, until he put all tariffs other than those on Chinese goods on hold for talks to take place. Taiwan and the United States on Friday held their first direct talks about the tariffs.

TSMC, which announced last month an extra $100 billion investment in the United States, is central to the U.S. chip industry, and bringing more of its production to U.S. soil would solve a major supply chain risk for customers that also include Qualcomm and Advanced Micro Devices .

Sam Konrad, investment manager for Asian equities at Jupiter Asset Management, said any geopolitical issues with Taiwan would create an extremely big problem for the U.S. tech companies that rely on the Taiwanese companies.

"So we don't think the Taiwan risk is priced into U.S. tech valuations, but we think it is priced into Taiwanese tech valuations," Konrad said.

data.title: Westpac Sees Little to No Effect on Consumer Spending to Date From US Tariffs
data.description:
Westpac Banking's card data suggest the announcement of tariffs by the US has had little or no effect on consumer spending to date, according to a Thursday report.

The US tariff announcement on April 2 resulted in a sharp weakening and spike in volatility across financial markets as well as a material decline in consumer sentiment.

The Westpac-DataX Card Tracker Index, which presents indicators based on the millions of credit and debit card transactions processed by the bank every day, slipped one point lower over the two weeks to April 12 to 140.3, comfortably above the 138.1 average since the start of the year.

The index continues to hold at relatively elevated levels after rebounding strongly from weather disruptions in March, the bank noted. Quarterly growth momentum has been largely unchanged over the past two weeks.

Card activity has been tracking a firmer up-trend since mid-2024, running at a 3.4% annual pace compared with the flat trend over the previous two years. This reflects support from both tax cuts and as well as recent decreases in interest rates.

There is a slight softening in quarterly momentum in recent weeks, centered on discretionary categories, per the reportt. Durables slipped back after a strong second half to 2024, while hospitality and travel also experienced a drag.

data.title: Gamuda's Renewable Push in Australia Likely to Support Long-Term GrowthMarket Talk
data.description: Gamuda's growing renewable energy business in Australia is likely to support its long-term growth on the back of Australia's green transition, RHB Investment Bank analyst Adam Bin Mohamed Rahim says in a note. Its unit DT Infrastructure's early work for the Carmody's Hill Wind Farm project in South Australia may pave the way for larger construction contracts. Meanwhile, Gamuda's construction revenue growth in Malaysia is likely driven by data center, rail and hydropower projects, potentially supporting higher net margins, the analyst says. RHB maintains a buy rating on Gamuda and keeps its target price at MYR5.83. Shares are 0.3% lower at MYR3.88.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

Vulcan Steel was the best performer in the sector, adding 0.4%.

On the other hand, consumer durables shares dropped past 1%.

Trump administration says Harvard may lose ability to enroll foreign students
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump has threatened universities with funding cuts over pro-Palestinian protests that he calls antisemitic
  • Human rights advocates have raised free speech and academic freedom concerns
  • Harvard rejected Trump administration demands this week
  • US government froze more than $2 billion in funding for Harvard

The U.S. Department of Homeland Security said Harvard University will lose its ability to enroll foreign students if it does not meet demands from the Trump administration in the government's latest escalation against the educational institution.

Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem also announced on Wednesday the termination of two DHS grants totaling over $2.7 million to Harvard.

Noem said she wrote a letter to Harvard demanding records on what she called the "illegal and violent activities" of Harvard's foreign student visa holders by April 30.

"And if Harvard cannot verify it is in full compliance with its reporting requirements, the university will lose the privilege of enrolling foreign students," Noem said in a statement.

Harvard did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

President Donald Trump's administration has threatened universities with federal funding cuts over pro-Palestinian campus protests against U.S. ally Israel's devastating military assault on Gaza after a deadly October 2023 attack by Palestinian Hamas militants.

Trump casts the protesters as foreign policy threats who are antisemitic and sympathetic to Hamas. Protesters, including some Jewish groups, say the Trump administration wrongly conflates their advocacy for Palestinian rights and criticism of Israel's actions in Gaza with support for extremism and antisemitism.

The Trump administration is also attempting to deport some foreign protesters and has revoked hundreds of visas across the country.

"With a $53.2 billion endowment, Harvard can fund its own chaos - DHS won't," Noem said, adding an "anti-American, pro-Hamas ideology" existed at Harvard.

Harvard has previously said it worked to fight antisemitism and other prejudice on its campus while preserving academic freedoms and the right to protest.

The Trump administration said late last month it was reviewing $9 billion in federal contracts and grants to Harvard and later called for restrictions - including a mask ban and removal of diversity, equity and inclusion programs - to be put in place for the university to continue receiving federal money.

Harvard on Monday rejected numerous demands that it said would cede control to the government. The Trump administration subsequently said it was freezing $2.3 billion in funding.

Trump also threatened on Tuesday to strip Harvard of its tax-exempt status. CNN reported on Wednesday the U.S. Internal Revenue Service was making plans to rescind the tax-exempt status of Harvard and that a final decision was expected soon.

Human rights advocates have raised free speech and academic freedom concerns over the crackdown by the government.

The Trump administration has frozen or canceled some funding for universities like Columbia, Princeton, Brown, Cornell and Northwestern as well.

It has also threatened to withhold funding over culture war issues such as DEI programs and transgender policies.

Rights advocates have also raised concerns about Islamophobia and anti-Arab bias during the Israel-Gaza war. The Trump administration has not announced steps in response.

data.title: Market Chatter: Japan Brokerages May Face Stricter ID Rules After Hacking Wave Hits Client Accounts
data.description:
Japan's securities association is pushing brokerages to adopt stricter multi-factor authentication for client accounts after recent hacking incidents enabled unauthorized stock trades, according to Bloomberg News, citing the association's top official.

Japan Securities Dealers Association Chairman Toshio Morita said Wednesday that an industry working group has begun drafting revised security guidelines to combat fraud, according to the newswire.

The move aims to mandate verification systems combining at least two identification elements for all online trading accounts, the report added.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Thailand's Brewing Industry Gets a Boost with Life Below Labs' Local Yeast Hub
data.description:
BANGKOK, Apr 17, 2025 - (ACN Newswire) - Life Below Labs, an emerging yeast microbiology startup in Southeast Asia, has officially launched its first production facility in Bangkok, Thailand. Doubling as the company's headquarters and central production hub, the state-of-the-art lab is dedicated to the innovation, preservation, and large-scale cultivation of fresh brewer's yeast - offering local craft breweries unrivaled access to high-quality, locally produced fermentation cultures.

The facility is equipped with cutting-edge yeast cultivation and quality control systems, including deep freeze storage that adheres to the rigorous standards of national microbial culture banks. This robust infrastructure allows Life Below Labs to preserve yeast cultures at a professional-grade level - ensuring a reliable supply of fresh, high-performance yeast customized to meet the evolving needs of Southeast Asia's craft beer industry.

Engineered for the production of commercial-grade fresh yeast, the lab empowers brewers with superior fermentation consistency, enhanced cell viability, and greater precision in shaping the flavor and aroma profiles of their beer - delivering a distinct edge over conventional dry yeast alternatives.

Yeast plays a pivotal role in brewing, deeply influencing a beer's aroma, flavor profile, and mouthfeel. However, access to fresh, high-quality yeast remains limited in Southeast Asia - hampered by import regulations, supply chain constraints, and the high costs of sourcing from overseas markets like the U.S., Europe, and Australia. Life Below Labs is breaking down these barriers by offering regionally produced, premium-grade fresh yeast specifically crafted to meet the needs of Southeast Asia's growing craft beer industry.

"This facility opens the door for brewers to push creative boundaries and redefine what's possible in craft beer," said Alan Edwards, Founder of Life Below Labs. "Fresh, high-quality yeast is a game-changer - enhancing flavor complexity, improving fermentation control, and ensuring batch-to-batch consistency. Our mission is to empower breweries across the region with the tools they need to innovate boldly and deliver exceptional, distinctive beers to their customers."

Backed by decades of combined expertise in bioprocess development and scale-up, Life Below Labs brings deep specialization in fresh yeast fermentation to Southeast Asia's brewing scene. Founder Alan Edwards and business advisor Thanit Apipatana, both graduates of Imperial College London with backgrounds in Bioengineering and Biochemistry, respectively, lead the team.

Trained to uphold rigorous quality standards, the Life Below Labs team ensures meticulous process management at every stage. Its professionally curated yeast bank provides breweries with customized, high-performance yeast strains - offering a dependable backbone for crafting a wide variety of alcoholic beverages.

Thanit Apipatana, business advisor at Life Below Labs, said, "Brewing is evolving rapidly, and innovation starts with the right foundation. At Life Below Labs, we view yeast as more than just an ingredient; it's the essential starting point for every exceptional beverage. We are dedicated to refining this foundation now, enabling brewers to experiment, create, and push boundaries later on without compromise."

Life Below Labs is currently focused on innovating new yeast strains for the future.

About Life Below Labs

Life Below Labs is a Thailand-based company that provides freshly propagated yeast solutions for craft breweries and beverage producers across Southeast Asia. Combining advanced microbiology expertise with innovative bioprocessing techniques, Life Below Labs delivers high-quality yeast tailored for superior flavor, consistency, and production flexibility. The company is led by founder and CEO Alan Edwards, who brings over a decade of microbiology experience to support breweries in creating exceptional beverages.

Source: Life Below Labs

Copyright 2025 ACN Newswire . All rights reserved.

data.title: Market Chatter: BOJ's Ueda Warns US Tariffs May Force Policy Shift, Rate Hikes Could Pause
data.description:
Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda signalled potential policy adjustments if US tariffs significantly damage Japan's economy, suggesting the central bank may halt its rate-hike cycle, Japan's Sankei reported Wednesday, citing an interview.

Risks from Trump's trade policies have increasingly aligned with the BOJ's "bad scenario" projections since February, already weakening business and consumer sentiment, Ueda reportedly said.

While maintaining plans for gradual rate increases if economic conditions permit, the governor emphasized readiness to act should tariff impacts intensify, the report added.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.
